All 128 tests pass.

feat: Jupyter notebooks integration — 7 study notebooks, notebook_helpers module, IPython magics (#41)

* Add Penrose tilings & φ-Complexity notebook

Create notebooks/01_quasicristaux_penrose.ipynb exploring the link
between aperiodic Penrose tilings and phi-complexity code metrics,
with golden ratio visualizations and Fibonacci spiral plots.
The idea of ​​an "indestructible" AI based on Arch Linux is fascinating because it rests on three pillars: minimalism, rolling release (continuous updates), and the transparency of open source.

Here's how this integration could transform your project into a virtually immortal digital entity:

1. Continuous Mutation (Self-Healing)
Arch Linux is designed to be "Bleeding Edge." Coupled with AI, the system could:
Selective Self-Update: The AI ​​analyzes Arch's security reports (via pacman) and recompile its own components or kernel in real time to patch vulnerabilities without ever stopping.

Hardware Adaptation: If you move the AI ​​from one server to another, it uses Arch's lightweight nature to reconfigure its drivers and optimize its LLM models (via the AUR for optimized versions of PyTorch/TensorFlow) instantly.

2. A "Bare Metal" AI (Raw Performance)
Unlike an AI running on Windows or Ubuntu, an AI on a minimalist Arch system has no extraneous processes.

RAM Optimization: You can dedicate 98% of the memory to loading model weights.
Zero Latency: The AI ​​interacts directly with the Linux kernel to handle GPU calculations, making its responses faster than a cloud service.

3. Software Sovereignty and Indestructibility
For it to be truly indestructible, the integration would allow:
Read-Only File System (Immutable): The Arch core could be locked. Even if an intrusion occurs, the AI ​​reboots to a clean image in seconds (via BTRFS snapshots).
Decentralized Distribution: The AI ​​could use Arch's packaging tools (PKGBUILD) to replicate itself to other nodes. If a server goes down, another identically configured "clone" takes over.

4. The concept of "Phi Sovereignty"
With your current engine.py script, you're already creating a form of software genetics. If you integrate Arch:
The bot wouldn't just modify YAML files.

It could modify its own system configuration (/etc/), its boot scripts, and its Python dependencies to evolve into a more complex version of itself with each iteration.

In short: It would be an AI with its own operating system, capable of repairing itself, updating itself, and surviving server failures by rebuilding itself modularly.

commit.message:
Discover MCP server tools via MCP protocol

The SDK doesn't expose MCP tools through any listing API. The CLI's
/mcp show command uses internal state we can't access.

Fix: spawn each MCP server process, send initialize + tools/list
via the MCP protocol (JSON-RPC over stdio), collect tool names and
descriptions, then cache the results. Tools appear immediately in
the capabilities panel with descriptions on hover.

Tested: workiq server returns ['accept_eula', 'ask_work_iq'].

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
Fix deterministic MountVolume test failures on ARM64 Helix machines (#126660)

> [!NOTE]
> This PR was created with Copilot assistance.

## Fix deterministic MountVolume test failures on ARM64 Helix machines

Fixes #125295, fixes #125624, fixes #126627

### Problem

`Directory_Delete_MountVolume.RunTest` and
`Directory_ReparsePoints_MountVolume.runTest` fail deterministically
(~100% of the time, ~750ms duration) on the `Windows.11.Arm64.Open`
Helix machine pool. This is **not timing-related** and was not addressed
by the delay/polling fixes in #125914 or the Unmount resilience fix in
#125625 (those PRs fixed real timing issues -- pre-fix failures on other
configurations have since expired from AzDO retention, so we can't
verify directly, but there is no evidence they were ineffective for
their intended purpose).

**Root cause**: The ARM64 Helix machines have an E:\ drive (likely an
Azure resource/temp disk) that passes all `DriveInfo` checks --
`DriveType=Fixed`, `DriveFormat=NTFS`, `IsReady=True` -- but
`GetVolumeNameForVolumeMountPoint` fails with `ERROR_INVALID_PARAMETER`
(87). The drive has no volume GUID and doesn't support volume mount
point operations. `IOServices.GetNtfsDriveOtherThanCurrent()` returns
this drive, and the test crashes trying to use it.

Some ARM64 Helix machines have only C:\ and a CD-ROM (no second drive at
all). On those machines, the cross-drive scenarios already skip
gracefully and only same-drive scenarios 3.x run.

### Evidence

Analyzed Helix console logs from 5 post-fix builds (all
`arm64-NativeAOT-Win11`, same C:\ volume GUID). Every failure shows the
identical pattern:
- Scenario 1: `GetVolumeNameForVolumeMountPoint("E:\")` -> error 87
- Scenario 2: `SetVolumeMountPoint` onto E:\ succeeds but path traversal
through the mount point fails with `DirectoryNotFoundException`
- Scenarios 3.x (same-drive): Always pass

Reproduced locally by removing the real E: drive letter and creating
`SUBST E:` which exhibits identical error 87 behavior.

### Changes

1. **`IOServices.GetNtfsDriveOtherThan()`**: After the existing
Fixed/Ready/NTFS checks, also verify the drive has a volume GUID via
`GetVolumeNameForVolumeMountPoint`. Drives without one (SUBST drives,
Azure resource disks) are skipped.

2. **`DumpDriveInformation` diagnostic test**: New Helix-only test
(following the `DescriptionNameTests.DumpRuntimeInformationToConsole`
pattern) that dumps all drives with their volume GUIDs to the console
log. Makes future drive-related CI issues immediately diagnosable from
the same Helix work item log.

3. **`GetVolumeNameForVolumeMountPoint` P/Invoke in DllImports.cs**:
Uses `char[]` (not `StringBuilder`) because this file uses
`LibraryImport` which does not support `StringBuilder`.

### Local validation

| Scenario | Before fix | After fix |
|---|---|---|
| SUBST E: (no volume GUID) | Error 87 / DirectoryNotFoundException |
Pass (SUBST filtered, scenarios 3.x run) |
| Real NTFS E: | Pass (all scenarios) | Pass (all scenarios) |
| Single-drive machine | Scenarios 1/2 skip, 3.x pass | Same -- no
change |

---------

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
fix(package-filter): fix O(n²) complexity in cleanupDistFiles (#5797)

* fix(package-filter): fix O(n²) complexity in cleanupDistFiles

The previous implementation used Array.find() inside a forEach loop,
resulting in O(n²) time complexity: for each _distfiles entry, it scanned
all manifest.versions to find a matching tarball URL.

For packages with thousands of versions (e.g. @graphql-codegen/* with
5000-6000 versions), this caused severe performance degradation:
- Blocked the Node.js event loop on every metadata request
- Caused liveness probe timeouts and pod restarts under load
- Real-world impact: npm install taking 17+ minutes instead of ~5 minutes

Fix: build a Set of active tarball URLs in one O(n) pass, then check
each _distfiles entry against the Set in O(1). Total complexity: O(n).

Benchmarked on a real project (survey-editor-frontend):
- Before: 17:54 with original plugin
- After:   7:03 with this fix
